skill.name: google-maps-search-api-skill
skill.description: Use BrowserAct to run Google Maps search workflows and collect local business data from search result lists.
skill.summary: Collect Google Maps business leads through the BrowserAct API.
skill.author: browser-act

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged a critical combination of network access, credential access, and command patterns. Manual review found the network and credential access are expected BrowserAct API behavior, while the shell, crypto, and reconnaissance hits are false positives from markdown text and ordinary documentation. Risk remains medium because user queries and a bearer token are sent to a third-party API.

medium_findings.title: Credentialed Requests to BrowserAct API
medium_findings.description: The script reads BROWSERACT_API_KEY and sends it as a bearer token to the declared BrowserAct workflow API. This matches the skill purpose, but it shares user search terms and credentials with a third-party service.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct requests calls use the declared BrowserAct endpoint and a bearer token from BROWSERACT_API_KEY. The behavior is intended, so this is a privacy and credential-handling risk rather than confirmed malicious exfiltration.

low_findings.title: Markdown Command Examples Misidentified as Shell Backticks
low_findings.description: The SKILL.md static hits are markdown inline code and a bash usage example. I found no executable Ruby-style backtick substitution or dynamic command construction in that file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited content is visible markdown documentation and a literal command example. There is no evidence that SKILL.md itself executes commands or interpolates user input into shell syntax.

low_findings.title: Crypto and Reconnaissance Findings Are Textual False Positives
low_findings.description: The cited lines contain ordinary documentation, field names, and error checks. I found no cryptographic algorithm use, system probing, network scanning, or hidden reconnaissance logic.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows descriptive prose and simple authorization error handling. No security-sensitive primitive or reconnaissance operation is present at those locations.

dangerous_patterns.title: Third-Party HTTP API Calls
dangerous_patterns.description: The script posts workflow inputs and polls task status through requests against the BrowserAct API. Users should expect outbound network traffic for each run.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The URL and requests calls are explicit in the Python script. The destination is the stated service provider, so the pattern is expected but security-relevant.

dangerous_patterns.title: Environment Variable Credential Access
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill requires BROWSERACT_API_KEY and the script reads it from the environment before making API calls. This is normal for API tooling but requires secret handling care.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The required environment variable is declared in metadata and read directly by os.getenv. The script does not print the key value, but it does transmit it as an Authorization header.

content.value_statement: Local business research often requires repeated Google Maps searches and manual cleanup. This skill runs a BrowserAct workflow to collect structured place data for review and outreach.
actual_capabilities: Runs a BrowserAct Google Maps workflow template from a Python script.
Accepts search keywords, language, country, and maximum result count.
Uses BROWSERACT_API_KEY from the environment for authentication.
Polls BrowserAct task status and prints timestamped progress logs.
Defines output handling for names, addresses, ratings, reviews, and related place fields.
limitations: Requires a valid BrowserAct API key and outbound network access.
Sends search terms and workflow inputs to the BrowserAct service.
Does not validate keyword, country, language, or result count inputs.
Current script control flow can time out before returning final results.

use_cases.title: Build Sales Prospect Lists
use_cases.description: Collect business names, addresses, ratings, and review counts for a target category and city.

use_cases.title: Compare Local Competitors
use_cases.description: Search competitor locations across a region and review visible Google Maps attributes.

use_cases.title: Prepare Location Datasets
use_cases.description: Gather place records that can be cleaned before import into a CRM or analysis spreadsheet.

prompt_templates.title: Search One Category
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for a single city and business type.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find coffee shops in Austin, Texas. Use English, United States results, and limit the search to 50 places.

prompt_templates.title: Collect Prospecting Leads
prompt_templates.scenario: Sales workflow with a clear export goal.
prompt_templates.prompt: Search for dental clinics in Phoenix. Collect names, addresses, ratings, review counts, and snippets for CRM review.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Markets
prompt_templates.prompt: Run separate searches for coworking spaces in Berlin, Paris, and Madrid. Keep language and country settings appropriate for each market.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Competitive Analysis
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced request that needs structured interpretation after collection.
prompt_templates.prompt: Find boutique hotels in Kyoto, review the returned ratings and price ranges, and summarize patterns for competitor positioning.

output_examples.input: Find Italian restaurants in Manhattan with up to 25 results.
output: A list of restaurant names with full addresses.
Ratings and review counts for each returned place.
Price range, cuisine type, and short review snippets when available.

output_examples.input: Research coworking spaces in Toronto for sales outreach.
output: A prospect list grouped by visible business details.
Progress messages while the BrowserAct workflow runs.
A note when the API key is missing, invalid, or expired.

best_practices: Confirm that BrowserAct data sharing is acceptable before running searches.
Use specific keywords and country settings to reduce irrelevant results.
Review returned place data before using it for outreach or CRM import.
anti_patterns: Do not paste API keys directly into prompts or shared transcripts.
Do not use broad searches when a narrow market or category is required.
Do not treat returned contact or review data as verified without review.

faq.question: What service does this skill use?
faq.answer: It uses the BrowserAct Google Maps Search API workflow through a Python helper script.

faq.question: Does it need an API key?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill requires BROWSERACT_API_KEY to be available in the environment.

faq.question: What data can it collect?
faq.answer: It targets place names, addresses, ratings, review counts, price ranges, categories, amenities, and review snippets.

faq.question: Can it run without network access?
faq.answer: No. It must contact the BrowserAct API to start and monitor workflow tasks.

faq.question: Is this safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes, with a warning. It is legitimate API tooling, but it sends searches and a bearer token to BrowserAct.

faq.question: Does the audit confirm malicious behavior?
faq.answer: No. Manual review found no prompt injection, malicious exfiltration, or hidden command execution evidence.
